subtasks.title: 1.1 Utilize IRC's Standard Country Program Structure as a guide to establish key roles and ways of working for the emergency response.

resources.name: Template Org. Chart (please use "Save As" before editing)

subtasks.title: 1.3 Create a prioritization plan for the local recruitment process.
1.4 Support the provision/creation of deployment TORs.
1.4 Work collaboratively with the Team Leads and the Deployment Management team to request and have visibility of the start and end of deployments.

1.5 Assess if Global Recruitment team or Regional Recruitment team may be able to assist with recruitment. If not, ensure recruiters are noted as part of the short-term staffing plan - utilize the GST to meet this need. Recommendation is 1-2 assigned recruiters.

1.6  Support the development of JDs, and review and approve long-term job positions while overseeing the recruitment process. In situations were the response location may be a challenging location in which to work and enter, please assess if the following note should be included in Job Descriptions open to expats: "The incumbent will be required to transit to and from [Country of Response] and must be able to acquire a local work authorization. Candidates that, for any reason, are not able to travel to and from [Country of Response] or who may be ineligible to work in [Country of Response] may not be eligible for this long-term role.”

tasks.title: Establish an R&R policy to apply to deploying and long-term hired staff members.

subtasks.title: 1.1 Reach out to the Global Mobility team to obtain hardship information of the new context in which we will operate. They will provide a suggestion. Confirm information with Response Lead.

1.2 Consider if there will be an R&R offer for national staff, or if it will only apply to international staff (depending on the context, need, and burden being placed on national staff - recommended to note this as a time-bound benefit, if offered)
1.3 Consider the particular access points or challenges that may impact the R&R schedule and adapt the policy as needed, with the wellbeing of our staff always remaining in focus.
1.4 Finalize and communicate R&R policy to incoming deployed staff as well as new employees.

tasks.title: Develop Staff Handbook for National Staff

subtasks.title: 1.1 Utilize the template, current IRC policies, and documentation provided by local labor counsel to establish an Employee Handbook for National Staff draft.
1.2 Review the stipulations of the draft Handbook with the Country Director -- come prepared to discuss what items are non-changable due to local labor laws.
1.3 Send drafted and reviewed Handbook to local labor attorneys for final review.

1.4 As needed, send Handbook for translation. Translation services may be provided by the local labor attorneys or we may utilize IRC's standard transaltion partners. Please ensure funding/codes are set aside for this process.
1.5 As needed, send finalized and translated Empoyee Handbook to the necessary Labor Ministry or Office for review and approval.

subtasks.title: 1.1 Conduct secondary data review (Review existing data, ensure inclusion of gender/disability/protection considerations, write a 2-page summary)

1.4.1 Review existing MSNA tools-  Examine all current MSNA tools and guidance documents. Verify if the language and content are appropriate for the local context and if any updates are needed.

1.4.3  Request support to deploy the MSNA toolkit on CommCare (If using a HH survey and mobile data collection) . Seek support from: - For an existing CP setup: Reach out to the Regional MEAL Technology Coordinator (RTC) team. - For a new location: Contact The EHAU MEAL Systems Advisor -Tom  .

tasks.title: Ensure Adequate Budgeting for MEAL Resources, Tools, and Capacity
tasks.responsible: ERT MEAL Coordinator  and STA MEAL

subtasks.title:
5.1 Allocate a sufficient budget for MEAL staff recruitment and salaries to support monitoring, evaluation, accountability, and learning activities.
- For new locations :
MEAL Technical Assistance & Staffing:
Recruit a dedicated MEAL Coordinator to oversee setup.
Hire a MEAL Officer per operational region to support data collection and analysis and CRA
Hire a CRA Officer at the national level to ensure accountability systems are in place.
Costs for technical support missions: USD 300 per day for personnel, plus travel-related costs (flights, lodging, per diem, visa fees).
Budget for technical assistance: External consultant support at USD 10,000 for 1 to 3 months to assist in setting up MEAL systems
5.2 Ensure funding for surveys, assessments, and data collection activities (e.g., baseline, midline, and endline evaluations).
5.3 Include budget provisions for training and capacity building for MEAL and program teams to enhance data quality and utilization.

5.4 Ensure adequate budget allocation for MEAL software and tools, including CommCare, Power BI, and other necessary platforms to support data collection, analysis, and visualization.
•Estimated Costs:
  1.Client Feedback Channels: Approx. USD 5,000 per year for telephone and SMS-based mechanisms.
 2. CommCare Licensing: USD 3,600 per year per sector, with a minimum of one CommCare space per country .
 3,  Hardware Needs: Approx. USD 200 per tablet for data entry personnel and USD 2,000 per laptop per field office.
4. MEAL Technical Assistance & Staffing: 
a)Country-level Information Management (IM) Manager at USD 30,000 per year (approx.) – For Existing CP.
b) External consultant support at USD 10,000 for 1 to 3 months for a national hire ( if needed)
c) Costs for technical support missions: USD 300 per day for personnel, plus travel-related costs (flights, lodging, per diem, visa fees).
Who to Consult: IT Team (software & hardware), Finance Team (budget approvals), Regional MEAL Technology Coordinators (technical guidance), and EHAU MEAL Team or RMA for Existing CP (proposal budgeting).

guidelines.title: Introduction
guidelines.content:
Emergency Management Guidelines 

Version 2.0 (changelog)

Updated: August 2025



TL;DR: These Emergency Management Guidelines define how the IRC delivers fast, scalable, and principled responses to crisis—anchored in the belief that decisions should be made as close as possible to affected populations. Country programs are in the lead, supported by an Emergency Unit that plays an intentional role in driving urgency, coherence, and ambition across the organization. Support is scaled to the needs and classification stance of each emergency—guided by a clear Response Imperative to act decisively when crises reach a defined threshold. The guidelines reaffirm our commitment to decentralized decision making, shared leadership with frontline actors, and a risk posture that collectively ensures we deliver for more people, more effectively, in a world under pressure.



IRC’s Evolving Emergency Posture 

The IRC’s emergency response model continues to evolve amid a shifting global landscape: more severe crises, constrained donor funding, and an increasingly crowded field of responders. The withdrawal of most U.S. government funding has forced many donors to reprioritize where and how they spend. As a result, more humanitarian need will go unmet than at any time in recent decades. At the same time, with donors continuing to push their remaining funds toward emergencies, other organizations are pivoting rapidly into emergency response. While this could benefit crisis-affected populations by bringing more expertise into the arena, it is likely going to create competitive chaos—a crowded, fast-moving landscape in which IRC’s ability to deliver with speed, scale, and quality will be what sets us up to continue helping people survive.



These guidelines reflect the expectation that IRC will respond to all orange and red classified emergencies —with country programs and the Emergency Unit collaborating closely to deliver humanitarian impact. This threshold establishes IRC’s Response Imperative: a clear organizational commitment to respond when the severity of a crisis demands it.



The Emergency Unit will contribute to this effort by setting global standards, providing targeted expertise, and deploying resources that enable country programs to respond quickly, coherently, and in ways grounded in frontline realities. Although the recent budget driven elimination of the Regional Emergency Director tier is a loss to the organization, remaining Emergency Staff look forward to collaborating directly with country leadership and teams. The Emergency Unit’s ability to flex resources quickly—whether through technical surge, operational troubleshooting, or targeted funding—will continue to be a critical offering to enable response teams to deliver timely, scaled and quality assistance.



The criticality of emergency response extends far beyond the immediate crisis. The quality and speed of our interventions in the earliest days of an emergency often define the trajectory of IRC’s programs—and presence—for years to come. Failing to deliver and transition timely, scaled, and principled responses will always undermine our ability to build sustained impact, support national systems, or secure the trust of crisis-affected communities and donors alike.



The challenge before us is clear: less funding, fewer colleagues, yet the largest number of conflicts since the Second World War and increasingly severe, climate-driven disasters. That means more people than ever in urgent need of our help. For the IRC to deliver lifesaving assistance quickly, at scale, and with quality, we will need to plan, collaborate, and support one another. These Emergency Management Guidelines set out the blueprint for how we will meet this challenge together.

guidelines.title: 1. Introduction & Overview
guidelines.content:
The IRC helps people whose lives and livelihoods are shattered by conflict and disaster to survive, recover, and gain control of their future. In humanitarian emergencies, that means delivering timely, principled, and effective responses—with decisions made as close as possible to affected communities. These guidelines define how IRC manages emergencies, clarifying roles and decision-making while allowing for flexibility in a dynamic world.



Purpose of This Document

The Emergency Management Guidelines provide long-term guidance on how IRC prepares for, launches, and supports emergency responses. Specifically, they:

• Reaffirm IRC’s commitment to humanitarian principles and response quality.

• Clarify decision-making across country, regional, and global levels.

• Define roles and responsibilities for coordination and accountability.

• Establish response standards that support scale, speed, and adaptability.



Shared Ownership and Responsibility

These Emergency Management Guidelines are issued jointly by the Vice Presidents for Emergencies and Program Delivery, underscoring that emergency leadership is a shared departmental responsibility. To embed this accountability across CRRD, job descriptions and performance goals for Regional Vice Presidents, Deputy Regional Directors, and Country Directors, will henceforth explicitly include emergency response leadership responsibilities, underlining that ownership of the IRC’s emergency ambition is carried across all levels of management.

guidelines.title: 2. Vision for Emergencies
guidelines.content:
The IRC will serve people affected by the most severe emergencies, with quality, responsive humanitarian aid. Our programs will be properly resourced, locally led, and always designed and delivered in partnership with local organizations and communities.

We will work in partnership with local organizations and communities to design and deliver programs that reflect what clients say they need most. Assistance will reach people quickly—even in hard-to-reach places—and will be tailored to the realities they face. We will invest in increasing the quality and reach of our programs, learning alongside partners as we go.

To deliver at scale, with quality and urgency, we must continue to recognize, develop, and empower emergency responders across our organization.

guidelines.title: 3. Defining Emergencies & IRC’s Response Approach
guidelines.content:
The IRC defines emergencies as emerging crises or rapidly deteriorating contexts in which existing services are overwhelmed, and basic needs go unmet. These are measured through IRC’s classification system. Most often, IRC responds to needs caused by conflict—whether people are forced to flee or shelter in place. We also respond where the impacts of climate change intersect with conflict, including when that leads to food insecurity, displacement, or loss of life-sustaining assets.

In emergencies, the IRC aims to act quickly and in alignment with local capacities. Country teams lead wherever possible, ensuring our actions are rooted in the needs and priorities of affected people. The Emergency Unit serves as an objective partner—understanding needs, advising on response scale, and providing support calibrated to the emergency stance.

The Emergency Unit leads all emergency responses in contexts where the IRC has no existing operational presence. In countries where IRC is already present, the Emergency Unit may also lead responses when the scale, speed, or complexity of the emergency requires a specialized and standalone structure. This includes high-risk or politically sensitive environments where a parallel response configuration ensures greater agility, neutrality, or access. In all cases, the Emergency Unit and Emergency Response Team operate in full compliance with the policies and approaches outlined in these Emergency Management Guidelines.

For more information on the IRC's response, approach, and models, see this expanded overview.

guidelines.title: 4. The Role of the Emergency Unit
guidelines.content:
The Emergency Unit is made up of specialists drawn from across the IRC’s technical, operational, and financial teams, brought together with a single focus: to help the organization move quickly and coherently in moments of crisis. Its design reflects lessons learned over decades of emergency response—about the need for speed, clarity, and decisions that hold under pressure. By connecting expertise across departments, the unit helps IRC act as one: coordinated, principled, and responsive. In a moment of structural change, the Emergency Unit models what it means to stay agile, reduce friction, and deliver for clients in crisis.

Working in close partnership with country and regional teams, the Emergency Unit supports preparedness and response through seven core functions:

Emergency Standard Setting: Develops and evolves standards and guidance for responsive, scalable, and high-quality emergency programming.

Crisis Monitoring & Classification: Maintains systems to detect emerging risks and issue classifications that trigger timely action.

Emergency Fund Management: Administers critical funding streams aligned to global emergency response priorities.

Leadership in Geographies New to the IRC: Leads responses in countries where IRC does not operate, or, when requested, in hard-to-reach areas of existing programs.

Surge Deployment: Mobilizes staff to support country-led responses and, in exceptional cases, leads major responses.

Organizational Convening: Coordinates organization-wide alignment in large-scale emergencies to drive urgency and coherence.

Capacity Sharing: Builds leadership and learning by exchanging technical and operational know-how with frontline teams.



These functions are delivered through five integrated teams within the Emergency Unit:

Emergency Response Team (ERT) – Deployable experts drawn from across technical and functional areas providing direct response support.

Quality in Emergencies – Drives program quality through standards, technical guidance, and performance monitoring.

People and Operations in Emergencies – Supports people, access, safety, and supply chain systems during emergencies.

Finance and Fundraising in Emergencies – Aligns and allocates funding, grants, and financial management with response needs and donor requirements.

Global Crisis Analysis – Monitors global trends and maintains the IRC’s classification system, enabling scenario planning and strategic response positioning.



See Emergency Unit organizational chart

guidelines.title: 5. Preparedness and Anticipatory Action
guidelines.content:
The Emergency Unit has updated its approach to preparedness based off of learning from Phase 1 of S100 by simplifying the guidance and aligning standards with existing expectations for country programs.  This simplified checklist will support Watchlist countries to achieve a minimum standard or readiness for emergency response and this process for completion and support will follow the SAP with support from the Director of Program Quality and Performance.  In addition to this new approach to preparedness, the Emergency Unit is introducing a combined package on Security, Access, Analysis, and Preparedness. Piloted in South Sudan this evolving approach will be available to all countries and prioritize support targeting the top ten Watchlist countries, and in new crisis situations (brief will be finalized in FY26). 

Where climatic hazards such as floods or droughts are forecasted with sufficient lead time, and where they are projected to occur in a fashion in-keeping with the IRC’s ‘Who We Serve and Where We Work’ guidance, the Anticipatory Action Team will work with country teams to conduct hazard-specific scenario planning and support pre-crisis activation planning alongside and in coordination with the Emergency Unit preparedness support.

guidelines.title: 6. Scalable Response Management Approach
guidelines.content:
The IRC’s Response Management Approach ensures that country-led responses receive timely and appropriate support, tailored to the severity of a crisis. Classification stances—Yellow, Orange, and Red—determine the type and intensity of Emergency Unit engagement, and adaptation allowed within IRC systems. For crises classified as Red, Regional Vice Presidents will provide written approval of the classification decision. This expands the earlier “Red 10” requirement and ensures that the highest-stakes emergencies are visible to and owned by regional leadership. The Vice President for Program Delivery is copied on all such approvals to maintain alignment across CRRD leadership.





This graduated model is designed to uphold local leadership while enhancing available capacities, activating policy adaptations and enabling IRC to scale effectively, act quickly, and deliver principled responses in rapidly evolving crises.

For further operational detail, refer to:

Quality in Emergencies Technical Assistance Model, Partnership in Emergencies Protocols [under development – to be released before the end of 2025], Crisis Response Fund Guidelines, and Operational Adaptations Guidance [ERRP].

guidelines.title: 7. Emergency Program Menu
guidelines.content:
With humanitarian funding declining and needs rising, the IRC must focus on where we can make the biggest impact in emergencies. A more streamlined approach will help us improve quality, speed, efficiency, and scale.

We’ve identified 14 priority emergency interventions that represent IRC’s strengths in acute crises. These were chosen based on evidence, feasibility, strategic importance, and potential impact. IRC provides global technical support and tools to ensure these interventions are delivered quickly and effectively.

This focused approach still allows flexibility to respond to different contexts. To support focus also within each response, we will shift to teams selecting a max of three sub-sectors at the start-up phase to build strong foundations for reach and fundraising. Internal Crisis Response Funds will be used only for Orange and Red responses, and only for interventions from this emergency menu. IRC will shift to only implementing from within the Menu whether the response is supported by internal CRF funding or not.

Where these priorities interventions align with I@S and P&L, the Emergency Unit will work with response teams, TUs, and Airbel to generate targeted learning—especially on cost-effectiveness, speed, scale, and quality. This learning will be gathered through existing data routines to avoid adding extra burden to teams.

As the interventions in this menu are driven by context and need, they do not affect SAPs or other strategy products — they will be reflected in the response plan itself.

guidelines.title: 8. Response Cycle
guidelines.content:
The Response Cycle highlights the most important steps and deliverables within an emergency response – it doesn’t seek to be exhaustive in outlining all activities undertaken by the response team but instead places emphasis only on those most critical for a successful, timely and scaled response.  

Phase

Process

Crisis Monitoring & Classification

Monitor emerging crises / deteriorating humanitarian contexts. 

Apply for an IRC emergency classification to assess crisis severity and response stance.

Go/No-Go + Response Launch

Determination made on whether IRC will respond (Go/No Go guidance and policy on Establishing New and Closing Operations), leveraging ‘Who We Serve, Where We Work’ guidance, and IRC’s Response Imperative.

Either:

Concept of Operations developed for countries new to the IRC. (Guidance on regional collaboration here. Template here, example here)

Country/Emergency Unit MOU initiated when ERT are deployed to support country led response.

If a country-led response, then a response plan based on the guidance below.

The response team issues Sitreps in Orange and Red stance emergencies. Sitreps are posted to the Rescuenet Crisis Hub.

Needs Assessment & Situation Analysis

Conduct multi-sector needs assessments to determine immediate humanitarian needs. 

Analyze affected populations’ priorities and vulnerabilities. 

Ensure assessments are inclusive, evidence-based, and coordinated / carried out with partners.

Conduct partner mapping.

Response Plan & Program Design

Develop a Response Plan aligned with IRC’s humanitarian commitments, Emergency Program Menu and response parameters guidelines (under development, to be released early July ’25).

Define programmatic priorities, delivery mechanisms, and partnerships goals. 

Determine Scale Targets for Orange and Red classified emergencies

Incorporate localization, safeguarding, and accountability measures.

Approval for Country led response is given by DRD and Emergency Unit Lead; for EmU led responses, the Emergency Unit Lead will approve following the receipt of ‘non-objection’ from the RVP.

Resource Mobilization & Fundraising

Develop response operating budget 

Request CRF funding (initial allocation possible for early activities and needs assessments). Refreshed FY26 Crisis Response Fund guidelines will be released in Q1 FY26.

Identify and engage private/statutory donors who are interested in the crisis to secure funding for emergency interventions. 

Develop and submit funding proposals based on Response Plan and Operating Budget.

Ensure timely allocation of resources to priority activities.

Implementation & Program Delivery

Deliver emergency programs efficiently and effectively. 

Ensure alignment with Response Plan, operational feasibility, security considerations, and community needs. 

Establish client feedback and accountability mechanisms to affected populations.

Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability & Learning (MEAL)

Track program performance and impact using established indicators. Leverage IRC’s Project Indicator Dashboard framework.

Conduct Real-Time Evaluations and After-Action Reviews (AAR) to capture key insights for future responses and adapt management.

Handover, Transition & Recovery Planning

Plan for transition from emergency response to recovery or long-term programming.

Engage local partners to connect emergency programs to longer term initiatives.



This structured approach ensures that IRC’s emergency responses are timely, well-coordinated, and driven by needs, with clear lines of responsibility at each stage of the response.  

The Emergency Management Guidelines introduce a Response Imperative: a default expectation that IRC will respond to all Orange and Red classified. For crises classified as Orange or Red, response teams are mandated to set a Scale Target of reaching at least 10% of the affected population. These are not hard rules—but they are intentional defaults, designed to spark timely, serious conversations about where we respond and at what scale. While some contexts may justify a smaller footprint—or no response at all—those decisions must follow from rigorous deliberation, not drift. The goal is to move decisively and adaptively: to start from a position of action, then shape each response based on ground realities, access, capacity, and what will deliver meaningful impact. See guidance on how the response imperative fits into the go/no-go decision-making process here.

guidelines.title: 9. Response Management Coordination and Oversight
guidelines.content:
Effective response management depends on structured leadership, strong coordination, and consistent oversight. These routines ensure that decision-making is fast, informed, and well-aligned across teams and functions.

Response Leadership Selection and Supervision

For emergencies classified as Orange or Red, the Emergency Unit assigns a senior leader—either a Senior Director or the Vice President for Emergencies—to serve as the Emergency Unit lead. This designation is made at the time of classification and is based on availability and bandwidth, ensuring immediate strategic partnership and coordination support is offered to the implicated country leadership team.

The Emergency Unit lead provides strategic guidance, practical support, and a direct link to Emergency Unit leadership team. Their responsibilities include coaching the Emergency Team Leader, facilitating problem-solving, convening and chairing the Emergency Response Management Team (ERMT), intervening to resolve bottlenecks, and ensuring alignment with/in headquarters. The lead is also responsible for completing SAP reporting requirements for the response. See here the Terms of Reference for the Emergency Unit Lead function.

Country programs may request an Emergency Team Leader—like any other deployable staff—through the Deployment Management System (DMS). These requests are routed to the Senior Director for People and Operations in Emergencies (Sr. Dir. POPS), who assigns appropriate personnel. When the Emergency Unit assumes direct oversight of a response, the Sr. Dir. POPS also drafts the Terms of Reference and processes the Team Leader request via DMS. Whether acting as Country Director or Field Coordinator, the Emergency Team Leader reports jointly to the in-country supervisor (normally Country Director) and to the assigned Emergency Unit lead for the duration of the deployment. See snapshot of how deployment requests are received and decisions on deployments are made here (derived from the ERT and GST manual).

Coordination 

During high profile emergencies, defined by IRC's approach to public communication and fundraising, the VP of Emergencies will convene all interested department focal points to a series of Crisis Coordination Calls to coordinate around a single set of updates from the country/response team, thereby increasing coherence in support, and reducing information requests toward the response team and country program leadership team.  See here the Terms of Reference for the Crisis Coordination Calls.



Emergency Response Management Team (ERMT)

The Emergency Response Management Team (ERMT) is IRC’s principal strategic-level oversight body for emergency responses requiring Emergency Unit support. Activated at the outset of Orange or Red classified crises, the ERMT provides cross-functional leadership, operational problem-solving, and institutional coordination to drive effective, accountable, and scalable emergency action. Chaired by the designated Emergency Unit Lead and including senior representatives from relevant EmU pillars, the region and country leadership, and CRRD, the ERMT ensures that support plans are matched to field realities, barriers to implementation are unblocked, and response strategies remain aligned with IRC standards and ambition. ERMT meetings follow a consistent agenda and cadence based on the maturity of the response, offering a structured space for collective decision-making, financial oversight, risk review, and performance tracking. It complements—not replaces—country-level SMTs and is critical to ensuring IRC delivers principled, timely, and outcome-driven humanitarian assistance in complex emergencies.  See here the Terms of Reference for the Emergency Response Management Team function.



VP Oversight of Emergencies

The Vice Presidents for Emergencies and Program Delivery will convene a monthly joint review of all Orange and Red classified emergencies. These sessions assess overall performance, identify support needs, and reinforce shared accountability for delivery across CRRD. Reviews draw on a standardized dashboard that tracks progress against Delivery Letter metrics—response imperative, client reach targets, speed to first service, and spending against CRF and other key awards. ERMT members and other senior staff may be invited as needed to support problem-solving or escalation.



CRRD SMT Quarterly Review

At the next tier of oversight, the CRRD Senior Vice President will convene a quarterly emergency review with the CRRD SMT. These reviews focus on the highest-priority emergencies—typically those classified as Red—providing space for Regional Vice Presidents, DRDs, and Country/Response Leads to present progress, challenges, and performance against Delivery Letter metrics. The quarterly review strengthens collective accountability, enables recognition of achievements, and provides a forum for troubleshooting and escalating barriers to timely and scaled delivery.
